front: What four elements must a valid Fourth Amendment warrant have?
back: (1) Probable cause; (2) supported by oath or affirmation; (3) presented to a neutral and detached magistrate; and (4) particularly describing the place to be searched and the persons or things to be seized.

front: A valid warrant needs probable cause, supported by {{c1::oath or affirmation}}, presented to a {{c2::neutral and detached magistrate}}, and {{c3::particularly describing}} the place and the persons/things to be seized.
back: oath or affirmation; neutral and detached magistrate; particularly describing

front: Johnson v. United States (1948) — who must draw the probable-cause inferences?
back: A neutral and detached magistrate, not the officer engaged in the often competitive enterprise of ferreting out crime.

front: Johnson v. United States (1948) — quote the rule on who decides probable cause.
back: Its protection consists in requiring "that those inferences be drawn by a neutral and detached magistrate instead of being judged by the officer engaged in the often competitive enterprise of ferreting out crime."
cite: Johnson v. United States, 333 U.S. at 13-14

front: Why does the warrant requirement put the probable-cause inference in a magistrate's hands (the Johnson principle)?
back: Because the officer hunting for crime is not detached; the Fourth Amendment's protection lies in having a neutral decisionmaker, not the investigator, draw the inference of probable cause.

front: Illinois v. Gates (1983) — what standard does the magistrate use to assess probable cause from an informant's tip?
back: The totality of the circumstances — a practical, common-sense judgment of whether there is a fair probability of crime.

front: Under Illinois v. Gates (1983), what is the role of an informant's veracity, reliability, and basis of knowledge?
back: They are relevant factors that inform the totality judgment — not rigid, independent, dispositive hurdles.

front: Franks v. Delaware (1978) — when must a warrant be voided and the fruits excluded?
back: When a knowing/intentional or reckless material falsehood, necessary to probable cause, is proven by a preponderance and the affidavit's remaining content is then insufficient to establish probable cause.

front: Franks v. Delaware (1978) — what must a defendant show to get a hearing on a false affidavit?
back: A substantial preliminary showing that a false statement was made knowingly/intentionally, or with reckless disregard for the truth, and that the statement is necessary to the finding of probable cause.

front: Franks v. Delaware (1978) — quote what happens to a warrant once the falsity is proven and the remaining affidavit is insufficient.
back: "[T]he search warrant must be voided and the fruits of the search excluded to the same extent as if probable cause was lacking on the face of the affidavit."

front: How does Franks v. Delaware relate to Leon's good-faith exception?
back: Franks is the first hole in Leon's good-faith shield: good faith does not apply where the magistrate was misled by an affidavit the affiant knew was false or recklessly disregarded the truth.

front: Maryland v. Garrison (1987) — on what information is a warrant's validity judged?
back: On the information reasonably available to officers when they sought the warrant; a reasonable, good-faith mistake about the premises does not invalidate it.

front: Officers got a warrant believing one apartment occupied the whole third floor, but it actually held two units; the search continued until the mistake became apparent. Which case, and what result?
back: Maryland v. Garrison (1987) — the reasonable, good-faith mistake about the premises did not void the warrant or the search conducted before the error became apparent.

front: Groh v. Ramirez (2004) — is a warrant that fails to describe the things to be seized valid if the supporting affidavit is particular?
back: No — such a warrant is facially invalid under the Particularity Clause, and a particular affidavit cannot rescue it.

front: Groh v. Ramirez teaches that particularity must live on the {{c1::face of the warrant}} itself — the {{c2::affidavit}} cannot cure a blank warrant.
back: face of the warrant; affidavit

front: Andresen v. Maryland (1976) — does a particularized warrant to seize a person's business records and use them in evidence violate the Fifth Amendment?
back: No — there is no Fifth Amendment compulsion problem because the accused is not compelled to do anything; nothing is extracted from him.

front: Andresen v. Maryland (1976) — quote the holding on searching/seizing business records and the Fifth Amendment.
back: "[T]he search of an individual's office for business records, their seizure, and subsequent introduction into evidence do not offend the Fifth Amendment's proscription that '[n]o person . . . shall be compelled in any criminal case to be a witness against himself.'"

front: Wilson v. Arkansas (1995) — how does knock-and-announce fit the Fourth Amendment?
back: The common-law knock-and-announce principle is part of the Fourth Amendment reasonableness inquiry, but it is not a rigid rule and yields to countervailing law-enforcement interests.

front: Richards v. Wisconsin (1997) — is there a blanket no-knock exception for felony drug cases?
back: No — there is no blanket exception by crime category; the no-knock decision is made case-by-case.

front: Richards v. Wisconsin (1997) — what must officers have to justify a no-knock entry, and on what grounds?
back: Reasonable suspicion that knocking would be dangerous, futile, or would allow the destruction of evidence.

front: Distinguish what Wilson v. Arkansas established about knock-and-announce from what Richards v. Wisconsin added.
back: Wilson made knock-and-announce part of the reasonableness inquiry (subject to law-enforcement exceptions); Richards held those exceptions cannot be applied as a blanket category — a no-knock entry needs case-specific reasonable suspicion.

front: Hudson v. Michigan (2006) — does a knock-and-announce violation require suppression of the evidence found inside?
back: No — the exclusionary rule does not apply to knock-and-announce violations; the interests it protects have nothing to do with the seizure of evidence.

front: Common pitfall: instructors assume a knock-and-announce violation suppresses the evidence. What is the correct rule (Hudson)?
back: It does not suppress evidence (Hudson v. Michigan). The entry may be unlawful for civil purposes while the seized evidence still comes in; the remedy is civil, not exclusionary.

front: United States v. Grubbs (2006) — are anticipatory warrants (effective only on a future triggering condition) constitutional?
back: Yes — anticipatory warrants are not categorically unconstitutional.

front: United States v. Grubbs (2006) — what two things must a magistrate find it presently probable will be true for an anticipatory warrant?
back: (1) The triggering condition will occur; and (2) once it does, contraband will be at the place to be searched.

front: Lo-Ji Sales, Inc. v. New York (1979) — why was the search invalid when the issuing magistrate led it?
back: An open-ended warrant executed by a magistrate who joined and led the search becomes a forbidden general warrant, and the magistrate loses his neutral-and-detached character.
cite: Lo-Ji Sales, Inc. v. New York, 442 U.S. 319 (1979)

front: Lo-Ji Sales, Inc. v. New York (1979) — quote how the Court described the issuing judge's loss of neutrality.
back: "He allowed himself to become a member, if not the leader, of the search party which was essentially a police operation. ... he was not acting as a judicial officer but as an adjunct law enforcement officer."
cite: Lo-Ji Sales, Inc. v. New York, 442 U.S. at 327

front: Coolidge v. New Hampshire (1971) — why was a warrant issued by the State Attorney General invalid?
back: Because as the chief investigator/prosecutor he was not neutral and detached; the issuing official must be detached from the investigation.

front: Lo-Ji and Coolidge each show a magistrate failing the neutral-and-detached test — how do the two failures differ?
back: In Lo-Ji, the issuing judge lost neutrality by joining and leading the search itself; in Coolidge, the issuing official (the State Attorney General) was never neutral because he was the chief investigator/prosecutor.

front: United States v. Leon (1984) — can a facially deficient warrant still support admissible evidence?
back: Yes — evidence obtained in objectively reasonable good-faith reliance on a warrant may be admissible even if the warrant is later found deficient, because suppression would not serve the exclusionary rule's deterrent purpose.

front: United States v. Leon (1984) — name the warrant-defects in this page that defeat good-faith reliance.
back: Good faith does not apply where the magistrate was misled by a knowing/reckless false affidavit (Franks), where the magistrate wholly abandoned the neutral role (Lo-Ji), or where the warrant is so facially deficient in particularity that officers cannot reasonably presume it valid (cf. Groh).
cite: United States v. Leon, 468 U.S. at 923

front: Particularity limits what officers may seize on sight under a warrant — how do items not described in the warrant come in, if at all?
back: Only under the Plain View Doctrine; the warrant's particularity clause does not authorize seizing items it does not describe.

front: Name the three ways a warrant fails when one of its elements collapses, with the anchoring case for each.
back: A falsehood corrupts the probable-cause showing (Franks); the magistrate abandons neutrality (Lo-Ji / Coolidge); or the description is open-ended or absent (Groh).
